data.title: Banco Macro: Why 2026 Matters More Than Any Quarter In 2025
data.description:
It's been quite a wild ride for Argentine equities as a whole, and the banking sector is no exception.

Banco Macro S.A.'s (BMA) ADR is currently down 15% year to date, at the moment I write this, even though it has rallied by more than 100% since the victory of Milei's party in the midterm congressional elections. Those guaranteed Milei's governability and the hope for progress on the anti-inflation and fiscal-discipline agenda.

But taking a step back, the BMA's still-weak result since the beginning of the year has been driven mostly by a confluence of macroeconomic factors rather than company-specific ones.

We can list for example some factors such as (1) global emerging markets flow turned strongly against Argentina in 2025, after strong appreciation in 2024, a natural profit-taking since the "Milei trade" at the end of 2023; and the (2) rise in Argentine sovereign risk (CDS) due to exchange rate uncertainty and residual political noise post-reforms with congressional elections (mid-term) bringing extra volatility.

More specifically about Banco Macro, it's important to note that this financial institution is basically a positive anomaly in the Argentine banking system, which makes it structurally different from Grupo Galicia (GGAL), BBVA Argentina (BBAR), Santander Río (SAN), and Banco Nación (state-owned).

Banco Macro dominates the inland provinces of Argentina (outside Buenos Aires), present in regional economies and agribusiness, which has historically given it higher spreads, more captive clients, and low competition.

In Argentina, Banco Macro is very strong in mass retail and micro/SME, with a low average ticket and simple products, which contrasts with Galicia and BBVA, with more diversified and corporate orientations. It has the lowest leverage ratio in the system and has the highest excess capital in Argentina by far (see graph below for H1 '25). This makes Macro the bank best positioned to consolidate the sector.

But understanding Banco Macro's competitive advantages only makes sense when we look at the bigger picture for Argentina in 2025.

In an Argentine macroeconomic scenario of a stressed exchange rate and falling inflation, what do banks need to do to ensure that the structural thesis doesn't break down?

In my view, and potentially that of the market, the correct answer would be something like keeping the structural pillars intact, even if the quarter's P&L comes in ugly.

After all, when inflation falls too quickly (1) the result per monetary position falls; (2) the NIM falls; (3) the gain on bonds falls; (4) the effect of indexations diminishes; (5) the quarterly ROE becomes a mess.

And when the exchange rate shoots up at the same time, the bank usually increases (1) provisions; (2) the cost of hedging; (3) the perception of risk, and (4) the P&L becomes even more compressed.

This shows that there's nothing pretty about a monetary transition in Argentina, where even excellent financial institutions will report bad numbers, and in scenarios of extreme noise, the market tends not to care so much about quarterly profits and margins, but rather structural pillars.

And that's basically what Banco Macro reported in its most recent quarter in 3Q25, anchored on (1) stable or growing deposits—solid funding; (2) excess capital to protect against volatility; (3) reasonably controlled NPLs, no collapse; (4) ample liquidity in ARS and USD and high cash; (5) no destructive regulatory changes, linked to the political risk of a more statist government— not the case today.

For these reasons, the bullish structural thesis on Banco Macro, and other Argentine Banks continues, and the re-rating remains anchored at 2026 with the ADR rising even with negative earnings.

A Cold Look at Banco Macro’s Tough 3Q25

Roughly speaking and coldly interpreting the figures, the most recent results released by Banco Macro showed a weak quarter, deteriorating in terms of net interest income, results per monetary position, provisions, and also costs. That's why we're going to go through all the grotesque figures in Banco Macro's latest balance sheet.

First of all, there is a clear pressure on the bank's core earnings. NIM fell from 23.5% to 18.7% YoY, the main negative driver for the quarter. Higher non-interest-bearing deposit requirements have put pressure on spreads, and a lower contribution from inflation (which previously inflated NIM) has caused core earnings to shrink. And all of this is the result of hypersensitivity to the Argentine monetary regime.

In the deposits line, there was an increase of 5% QoQ and 11% YoY, while loans rose 3% QoQ and 69% YoY—driven by the USD currency. But since there was a sharp drop in NIM, balance sheet growth did not translate into profit. This shows Banco Macro growing in funding and portfolio, but with compressed profitability.

There was also a noticeable deterioration in credit quality, with NPLs rising from 2.1% to 3.2% (+113 bps) YoY. This deterioration requires higher provisions and directly impacts the operating result. And it hits retail and SME credit risk the hardest, a point that could be hit further down the line if the recovery in 2026 is slightly slower than expected.

The inflation-adjustment results and net income from financial assets fell 84% QoQ and 86% YoY, highlighting a strongly negative monetary position result. Basically, the inflation-driven mechanics that used to deliver automatic gains from inflation and government bonds stopped helping the bank's bottom line (and even started hindering it).

Administrative expenses rose 20% YoY, despite the closure of 21 branches, which seems strange, but shows a consequence of wage pressures indexed to inflation. In terms of profitability, annualized ROE for 3Q25 closed at -2.6%, and for the last nine months at 4.5%, at least showing structural earning power, only totally suppressed by the macro environment with falling inflation and compressed spreads.

On the more positive side, the leverage of 4.3x assets to equity ratio is lower than domestic peers, and denotes an extremely conservative balance sheet, opening up two alternatives in the medium term (2025-26): (1) acquisitions (as the market speculates); and (2) extraordinary dividends and/or buybacks. Exposure to government bonds (SP/TP) was also moderately reduced to 22.3% of assets, showing a clear sign of reduced dependence on the sovereign.

Why the Structural Thesis Remains Intact

Even with the seemingly poor results, I anticipate that there are reasons to remain constructive on Banco Macro's thesis (including an immediate bullish reaction post 3Q25).

The rationale behind this seemingly disconnected optimism with the reality of recent figures is that the market is simply not buying the quarter or nine months of 2025, but rather 2026. And nothing in Q3 '25 breaks the structural thesis that next year is likely to bring.

I would put forward two phases to better explain how Argentine banks can start making money again when the economy stabilizes. The first is the transition from high inflation to falling inflation, and the second is stabilized low inflation and a normal banking cycle.

As the graph below shows, when Javier Milei took over the presidency of Argentina, inflation was 211% per year (December 2023), it fell to 118% in December 2024, and the trend is for it to continue falling to 28% in December 2025 and 14% in December 2026. First of all, this is fantastic because it effectively shows Argentina coming out of one of the worst inflationary regimes on the planet, which will lead to an improvement in people's real lives very quickly.

With inflation at ~1.5% per month (as analysts predict in 2026), three fundamental things happen at the same time: (1) the real NIM recomposes itself, as with stable inflation the Central Bank reduces the use of extraordinary borrowing, the spread between funding costs and assets normalizes, and most importantly banks can price credit using positive real interest rates; (2) the monetary position ceases to be negative, where the hyper-inflationary accounting effect disappears; and (3) credit is reborn, real personal loans flourish, leasing, mortgages, and so Argentine banks stop living off inflationary arbitrage and go back to living off real credit. In this specific case, this is where Banco Macro, which is strong precisely in retail + SME, tends to explode in profitability.

In other words, in 2026, the mere prospect that inflation will no longer fall at explosive speed, but will converge to a healthy level, is exactly the moment that will transform banks' P&L.

Valuation: Where the Alpha Still Lies

That said, as the market reacts based on expectations, aren't these expectations of stabilizing inflation already priced in?

At the moment, Banco Macro is trading at 149% of its book value, with the theory already embedded that (1) Milei is still in power and therefore inflation will continue to fall; (2) the worst of the operational disinflation is over; and (3) 2026 will be better than 2025.

But that's only the obvious part of "stabilization," and it doesn't exhaust the possible re-rating.

I would say that the alpha of the thesis is still guided by the quality and depth of economic stabilization in 2026, and signs that go beyond disinflation to strong and sustainable stabilization leave room for a re-rating, especially since Argentina is still a country with volatile political risk (although it fell after the mid-term elections) and the exchange rate regime has not yet been completely defined. So here, I see room for a re-rating in terms of normalization rather than merely disinflation.

Another point is the return on real credit, which is the biggest driver of ROE. A strong expansion of personal credit and the return of long-term SME credit are very powerful for Banco Macro. I doubt that once the bank starts posting better and better ROE figures, it won't set a precedent for a bullish reaction. If today's consensus can see ROE going to ~12% or ~13% by 2026, if inflation stabilizes at 14% YoY and 1.5% MoM, NIM returns, and credit revives, for every one percentage point of additional ROE, fair P/B rises.

Also, I would put the use of Banco Macro's excess capital (the largest in Argentina) as a competitive advantage in this case, added to a low leverage (assets to equity ratio), yielding M&As, buybacks, and capital reduction that today do not enter the 1.4x book value that the bank trades.

Risks and Final Remarks

Investing in Argentine equities is complex, involving high exposure to political risks and a constant anchor to falling inflation. But on the other hand, they are in an economy that is undergoing one of the fastest and most profound stabilization processes in its modern history.

Banco Macro is particularly well placed in this transition, as (1) it carries the largest excess capital in the Argentine banking system; (2) it has one of the strongest retail and SME franchises outside the Buenos Aires region; (3) maintains very low leverage; and (4) retains meaningful optionality for accretive capital deployment.

In this case, if inflation does indeed stabilize at low double digits in 2026, I anticipate that the banking sector will transition from survival mode to a normal banking cycle, and nothing can be more bullish than that, as Banco Macro's earnings power re-emerges almost mechanically.

So, considering that the BMA is not a call for the next quarter, but for the next regime, and the consensus model disinflation but arguably not normalization, I think the gap is where the upside lies.

data.title: Hormel Foods: Recent Dividend Raise, Yield At Decade Low - Bargain Or Value Trap?
data.description:
Introduction

In 2025, I've grown more cautious about certain sectors as inflation continues to place downward pressure on quality businesses.

Although many have continued to perform well, one particular consumer staple, Hormel Foods (HRL) continues to face challenges, as evidenced by the decade-low share price.

With staying power and a brand name, the Dividend King could be either a great bargain at the current price, or a potential value trap. But this depends on your time horizon and portfolio strategy.

In this article, I discuss Hormel's latest earnings, upcoming quarter, and why, despite the decade-low valuation and attractive yield, I'm rating the stock a hold.

Brief Overview

Most of us are likely familiar with the food giant, Hormel Foods. The Dividend King has a long operating history, having been founded in 1981. Their founder, George Hormel, had success with the sale of the company's products, opening its first branch in 1901.

Today the company is known for their brands like Jennie-O, Skippy, SPAM, Planters, and Hormel Chili. HRL operates in three segments: Retail, Foodservice, and International.

Disappointing Investment

As previously mentioned, Hormel has been a disappointing investment over the past year. At the time of writing, the consumer staple is down a sizable 26.53%.

When you compare them to peers like The Campbell's Company (CPB) and The J.M. Smucker Company (SJM), all three food stocks have underperformed in the past year. This can be attributed to a number of factors, but higher-for-longer inflation has likely been the main contributor.

But even when you look out over a 10-year period, HRL has been a disappointing investment, down nearly 37%. As a result, shares now trade near a 10-year low, as previously mentioned. Since hitting a 52-week low of $21.03 last month, the stock has seen some price appreciation, up roughly $2 to $23.23 at the time of writing.

Mixed Q3

For income-focused investors, HRL could be a potential bargain if they can successfully turn things around. During their Q3 earnings back in August, the company saw its share price slip as a result of disappointing earnings.

EPS amounted to $0.35, missing analysts' estimates by $0.06. This was flat from the previous two quarters, while sales were $3.03 billion, beating by $50 million. While sales managed to see slight growth of 1% year-over-year, their bottom line declined from $0.37 the previous year's quarter.

Through the first 3 quarters, sales were $8.92 billion, up from $8.78 billion, while EPS was down 9.5% from $1.16 to $1.05. HRL's earnings decline was attributed to lower cash flows & income due to higher inventory and an elevated commodity market.

Comparably, peer The Campbell's Company saw 6% sales growth for fiscal year 2025. But their bottom line declined 4% from $3.08 to $2.97.

Narrowed Guidance

Another factor for the low valuation is HRL's lingering headwinds forced management to lower its full-year guidance. While they do expect a slight increase in sales, operating income and earnings per share were revised lower.

At midpoint, EPS is expected to see an 8.9% decline from the $1.58 they saw in 2024. Operating income last year amounted to $1.1 billion. So, the company is expecting a slight decline.

As a result, margins have also experienced pressure. Last fiscal year, profit margins were 17%. In Q3, these were 16.7%. Management attributed the pullback to inflation as well as softness in the food market.

I think some of this can be attributed to the explosive popularity of GLP-1 drugs, which has forced food companies like Hormel to pivot.

To combat this, the company stated they look for brands like Jennie-O, Planters, and value business to increase profit margins going forward.

Upcoming Quarter

On a positive note, HRL is expecting to see some top & bottom line growth in the upcoming quarter next week. Sales are expected in a range of $3.15 billion to $3.25 billion, while earnings are expected to increase $0.03 on the low end.

So, for shareholders, this is positive. I expect the company to see continued pressure with earnings likely flat. Especially, since management stated they expect a $0.01 to $0.02 impact from tariffs. But if the company does manage to meet or exceed, I believe the share price will likely pop.

Dividend & Balance Sheet

Probably the most attractive metric about Hormel is the dividend yield of 5%. The giant raised their dividend earlier this week by 0.9% to $0.2925. While this is significantly lower than the current rate of inflation and is a testament to the company's current headwinds, this extends the Dividend King track record.

HRL has 97 years of uninterrupted dividends and has increased the dividend for 60 years. For Q3, HRL saw free cash flow of $85 million and paid out $159 million in dividends. Through 3 quarters, free cash flow was $303.3 million and $474 million in dividends.

So, despite current pressures, I think the dividend will remain safe, at least for the near term. In 2024, HRL ended the year with a free cash flow payout ratio of 59%.

The company's balance sheet also gives them flexibility with a low leverage ratio within management's targeted range of 1.5x - 2x. Total debt was $2.9 billion, relatively small in comparison to their market cap of $12.78 billion.

Bargain Or Value Trap?

At the current price, Hormel's price is appealing at a forward P/E of 16.1x. As you can see, the stock has fallen below its intrinsic value and normal P/E of nearly 20x. Using their normal multiple, the stock offers solid upside with a price target of roughly $32 over the next 2 years.

The question is, can the company return to growth in 2026? Next year Hormel Foods expects to see 7% bottom-line growth and 9% growth in 2027 according to FAST Graphs.

During Q3, management stated they expect headwinds to carry over into 2026. And although base rates have come down and are likely to provide tailwinds, I don't think HRL will start seeing positive impacts until the back half of the year at the earliest. So, the current earnings growth expectations could be a stretch.

In short, investors shouldn't expect much upside from the current price. But you do collect a 5% dividend yield while you wait. So, from an income and valuation standpoint, Hormel could be a good fit inside an income-focused portfolio.

Moreover, the economy seems to be weakening, as evidenced by the recent uptick in unemployment. If we do see further economic weakening, I expect profit margins to continue seeing downward pressure.

Although the current yield and valuation are attractive, I would like to see economic stability, margin expansion, cash flow growth, and how management executes on expected growth strategies before considering upgrading the stock to a buy.

Bottom Line

Hormel Foods will likely continue to see downward pressure at least for the next 2 to 3 quarters.

Although interest rates are likely to be lower in the next year or two, other factors like GLP-1 drugs, higher input costs, and tariffs are likely to linger, limiting any significant upside over the near to medium-term.

Although the company could be a solid long-term play with upside, economic uncertainty and margin pressures keep me at a hold for now.

data.title: Puma: Why I Don't Think The Acquisition Will Materialize
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I wrote about Puma SE (PMMAF, PUMSY, FWB: PUM) was back in August, when the Pinault family, which is a long-time owner of about 30% of Puma SE, was reportedly exploring a sale. The market got really excited, as the stock of this company has been deeply depressed this year, and investors were hoping for a recovery of the stock price as a result of a potential acquisition and a much-needed turnaround. The shares jumped more than 15% back in August, but I gave this stock a Hold rating and said it was a risky and speculative trade.

Now, a few months later, the same thing had happened, but this time, we got the news of two potential clients who are thinking of acquiring Puma SE. Puma trades in Frankfurt, so the stock made a major move there of more than 20% on Thursday (from ~15.5 euros per share to almost 20 euros), while U.S. markets were closed for Thanksgiving. You will see the reaction of ADR only after the market reopens in the U.S., but the stock on Frankfurt already pulled back slightly today, on Friday, before the U.S. market opens.

Before discussing the takeover news, it’s important to look at the actual condition of the business, and we’ve had a major update just a few weeks ago, so the information is relatively fresh. Puma’s third-quarter results came out late October, and just like the second quarter, they made it very clear that the company is far from the bottom. Last time I covered the stock, sales were falling almost everywhere except parts of Latin America and EEMEA. This quarter has essentially confirmed that the weakness is broad, structural, and affecting almost every region.

When you open the Q3 presentation, the regional breakdown itself is enough to see that it’s very early to talk about any kind of turnaround. EMEA declined 7.1% on a currency-adjusted basis. The Americas fell 15.2%, with North America down nearly 20%. The Asia-Pacific region dropped 9%, and Greater China alone contracted almost 20%. The only region that remains slightly positive is Latin America (you can understand that by the Americas' decline of 15.2%, while the northern part declined sharper than that).

I am surprised to see that the company decided not to add this to the presentation, mixing this picture with something positive. After all, preserving a “face” even in tough times is one of the important roles of the management, and it kind of looks like Puma gave up and decided not to even pretend that something goes according to plan.

Q3 2025 Earnings Results

When you look at product categories, the situation doesn’t look better than it does for the geographic sales breakdown. Footwear, which historically has been one of Puma’s strongest segments, declined almost 10%. Apparel fell nearly 13%, and accessories dropped more than 6%.

What makes the situation more difficult is the ever-growing inventory. The company ended the quarter with €2.124 billion in inventory, which represents a 17.3% increase from last year (the company chooses to compare with the worst quarter, instead of a year ago, which’s why it’s 19% on the slide). The inventory builds up quarter by quarter, and the accompanying commentary notes that there is simply too much product in the market (they refer to Puma’s warehouses, stores, wholesale partners, etc.).

I don’t know how well each of you is familiar with retail, but this situation is extremely bad. I saw something similar firsthand on a much smaller scale when my father was in this business. In a situation like this, you end up in a cycle that is very hard to exit, because the only way to get rid of that much stock is through major discounts and sales. Once you start giving your products away for very cheap, the brand loses pricing power, margins naturally compress, but what is even worse, the consumer begins to associate the brand with low value. It could sound like a bad place to be in, but it can get worse. It gets worse when you make major discounts, everything that is explained above happens, but you end up with a very high inventory nonetheless. Meaning, people don’t want your outdated products at all, not related to their pricing. Hopefully, it won’t come to that, because that way Puma will need to throw everything and take losses. As you can see from the slide, the company is aware of this issue, they expect an inventory peak in the next quarter, and only after that, slow improvement till the end of 2026.

Arthur Hoeld (a new CEO and a former executive at Adidas (ADDYY)) is slowly revealing his turnaround plan. The problem is that his plan essentially confirms everything Puma has done wrong for years. He openly stated that Puma has “become too commercial,” meaning the brand has been overexposed in discount-driven channels that dilute brand credibility.

He also said that the product offering is not “cutting through” and that the company is releasing too many items without establishing clear icons or long-term franchises. This is all visible throughout the presentation. This is probably the most negative presentation I have ever seen from a public company, which looks like an aggressive way to make a turnaround (which is more likely) or like giving up altogether. I think it’s a first option, because it’s not unknown when the company gets new management. They try to show how bad the situation is, to make a turnaround look better in the future. But we need not forget the acquisition side of the story, because it complicated the plan of the current CEO.

One particularly telling slide is where Puma displays its own internal “brand heat” tracker. The company puts itself very far below competitors (by the slide below, it looks like there is no weaker brand than Puma on the market), showing that consumers do not find the brand relevant. I would like to hear what you think, but it makes me really sad for some reason. I am not a person from the inside, so I don’t know whether they have some genius turnaround plan, but with the talks of acquisition and a presentation like this, I would not hope for a high valuation if I were a shareholder.

Hoeld’s plan for restructuring focuses on reducing the product range, integrating product, marketing, and sales teams (which have been working independently), cutting 900 additional corporate jobs on top of 500 earlier this year, and implementing stricter cost controls.

They present a major reshuffling of the “C”-level leadership. You can see new faces in departments from marketing to finance to supply chain and innovation. The CEO said that 2025 will be a reset year. 2026 will be a transition year, and there is no reason to wait for some meaningful recovery until somewhere in 2027, which is a long time from now, and of course, the turnaround is not guaranteed.

Puma has a 15.3% YoY decline in Q3 revenue, and gross margin fell by almost three percentage points. Adjusted EBIT went down from €237 million a year ago to €39.5 million this quarter. Reported EBIT, including restructuring costs, dropped even further to just €29.4 million.

Puma posted a net loss of €62.3 million for the quarter. The nine-month figures look even worse, with the company reporting a net loss of more than €300 million for the year-to-date period. Management also confirmed that Puma will report a full-year loss for 2025.

Acquisition Rumors

The acquisition narrative is the only part of the Puma that actually excites the market right now. According to sources, Anta Sports (a nearly $30 billion Chinese sportswear company) has been evaluating a potential bid for Puma and has already brought in advisers to evaluate the potential bid size. Li Ning, another large Chinese sportswear brand, is also discussing financing options with banks to assess whether a bid would make sense. In some reports, Asics was also mentioned, though the company later denied involvement. Investors are happy that a stronger, richer player might accelerate Puma’s brand recovery faster than Puma could do on its own, but what would be the price of the acquisition, given the presentation that we’ve seen before?

In general, the biggest obstacle to any acquisition is valuation. Despite Puma having lost more than 75% of its market value over the last five years, the Pinault family owns roughly 29% of the shares and has made it clear that they do not intend to sell at today’s depressed prices. Reuters mentioned this explicitly:

Artemis has said it is considering all options for its 29% stake, but a source close to the firm told Reuters in September it would not sell at the current market value , making it clear it is banking on a successful turnaround by Puma's new CEO Arthur Hoeld.

Buyers like Anta and Li Ning may be interested, but no acquisition can happen without Artemis agreeing to the terms, and at the moment, they appear to be waiting for the turnaround to materialize rather than exiting at a discount. I don’t expect buyers to be interested in paying a premium after the management presented their Q3 of 2025. Maybe that was one of the reasons for clearly stating all of the problems? To win more time for the turnaround before the Penault family agrees to sell? Don’t take these questions of mine at face value, though. I simply bring up my personal thought process, but it could be entirely wrong.

Any buyer would be inheriting a brand that is currently struggling in every important way. For a company like Anta, Puma may still be attractive because it offers immediate entry into Western markets and a long sports heritage. But buying Puma now means taking on the full weight of a troubled business, and acquisitions are never simple, even when talking of well-established businesses.

Valuation

Taking into account everything that we saw from the earnings presentation, the valuation doesn’t look too cheap. The TTM price-to-sales ratio is just 0.30, which is more than 67% below the sector median, but it doesn’t mean value for the situation this company is in. EV/sales is only 0.59, which is roughly half of where the industry trades. These discounts naturally look like an opportunity, but when you combine them with collapsing margins, negative earnings, shrinking brand appeal, and an extended turnaround timeline, it becomes clear that Puma is cheap for a reason. A low multiple alone does not guarantee that a stock is undervalued.

Conclusion

So, my opinion about this acquisition isn’t going toe-to-toe with the market’s reaction. On one hand, I don’t believe that the current management of Puma actually wants this acquisition to happen, because in my personal opinion, everything the company is doing right now points to management trying to set the stage for their own turnaround. I don’t think an acquisition makes sense for them at this moment. At the same time, the Pinault family has said that they will not be selling at current market prices, and they made it clear that they would want a significant premium for their stake.

Putting everything together - the fact that the presentation was really, really bad, and the results were shown from a very negative side, and that the Pinault family would demand a high premium - I personally don’t see a high chance of an acquisition happening at all. And the market reaction, in my view, comes only from the fact that investors are deeply in the red with this stock and are desperate for any kind of positive catalyst.

So I wouldn’t see this as a major investing opportunity at the moment. I won’t be changing my rating from Hold to Buy, because I don’t think that would make sense here, and overall, I would simply stay away from the stock for now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 3 Big Stock Discounts This Black Friday
data.description:
Black Friday is here, and we are all looking for discounts.

But unfortunately for investors, Black Friday is cancelled this year.

The stock market is currently trading at its highest valuation ever, according to the average of the following 4 valuation indicators:

  • Crestmont P/E from its geometric mean
  • Cyclical P/E 10 from its geometric mean
  • Q ratio from its geometric mean
  • S&P composite from its regression

This does not mean that the market is about to crash. Just that prices are high, which is likely to result in lower future expected returns.

The investment firm, GMO, is widely followed for its 7-year expected return predictions, and it currently expects US stocks to face a lost decade ahead, largely as a result of excessive valuations.

You will note, however, that they expect US value stocks and international equities to deliver much better results going forward, and this is simply because their valuations are far more reasonable.

The broader stock market hides pockets of weakness, with sectors like REITs still trading at historically low valuations:

At the same time, some international markets have completely missed out on the recent AI rally and continue to trade at much more reasonable valuations.

Therefore, this Black Friday, investors may want to look for opportunities in deep value stocks, niche sectors like REITs, and in foreign markets.

Here are three such opportunities that I am buying for my portfolio:

Caesars Entertainment (CZR): Deep Value

Caesars is what I would describe as a deep value play.

The consumer discretionary segment of the market has crashed hard this year due to fears that the consumer is weakening.

As a result, popular restaurant stocks like Chipotle (CMG), CAVA (CAVA), SweetGreen (SG), Wendy's (WEN), and Jack in the Box (JACK) are all down 50-80%:

Caesars focuses on an even more cyclical category, which is that of casinos, hospitality, and entertainment, so it is down even more than those restaurant stocks:

The market fears that if we face a recession, people will gamble less, leading to much lower profits for Caesars, which is one of the leading gaming companies in the US.

It owns about 50 casinos, including trophy assets on the Las Vegas strip such as the Flamingo and Paris:

And we think that it has become highly opportunistic following its 80% crash, given that it now trades at just 4x our estimate of its normalized cash flow.

Yes, that cash flow may decline in the near term if we face a recession, but that would be just temporary.

Besides, the company's digital gaming business is growing at a rapid pace, and the company expects it to hit $500 million of Adjusted EBITDA in 2026.

That's worth a lot of money considering that pure-play digital peers like DraftKings are trading at about 20x EBITDA.

Applying that multiple to $500 million would result in $10 billion of value from this business alone. Yet, CZR's current market cap is just $4.5 billion, which shows that not much value is given to its rapidly scaling digital business.

Bears will be quick to point to debt as a reason to avoid the stock, but I think it is less of an issue when you consider that it owns a big chunk of its real estate. We have previously estimated its real estate to be worth nearly its entire enterprise value, meaning that it could sell big chunks of it via sale and leaseback transactions in order to pay off debt if it wanted to. This makes me a lot more comfortable than if their entire casino portfolio were leased.

Ultimately, we think that Las Vegas will remain strong over the long run, and as it recovers and CZR keeps scaling its digital business, even as it also deleverages its balance sheet, the market will eventually reprice the stock at a much higher level.

If it fails to do so, CZR could always consider spinning off its digital business and/or selling more of its real estate to unlock value.

Carl Icahn is a big investor here and has publicly said that he likes the management. He bought at a nearly 2x higher levels, and not much has changed other than the recent recession fears.

We see fair value at about 3x higher levels.

It is a riskier investment, but the reward potential is substantial.

Armada Hoffler Properties (AHH): REITs

Most REITs are today discounted as they have suffered a three-year-long bear market.

But some REITs are more discounted than others, and Armada Hoffler (AHH) appears to be one of the cheapest in the entire sector, trading at just 6x normalized FFO, or put differently, it is offering a 16% FFO yield.

The reason why the cash flow yield of its stock is so high is that it trades at an estimated 50% discount to the net asset value of its properties.

In other words, its stock is offering you an opportunity to buy real estate at just half of its fair value.

What's the catch?

Does it own troubled assets? No. These are mostly trophy mixed-use properties in growing Sunbelt markets with high occupancy rates and steady rent growth:

Is the management conflicted? Again no. They own a lot of equity, and up until the pandemic crash, they had a long track record of significant market outperformance:

The catch is that the REIT is small, overlooked, and somewhat more leveraged than the average. It also has some exposure to offices and cut its dividend earlier this year, which have worsened its market sentiment.

This explains why it is trading at an even steeper discount than its peers.

But the leverage, while higher than usual, remains reasonable at 7.1x ,and it is declining rapidly as it finishes its development projects:

Moreover, its office buildings are generally part of highly desirable mixed-use properties that are performing well and enjoying long-term leases.

Therefore, we view this as a great opportunity for long-term oriented investors looking to buy real estate at a steep discount. The high 16% cash flow yield on its own should provide strong returns over time. The REIT is today paying a 9% dividend yield, and retaining the rest for deleveraging and reinvesting in growth.

We estimate fair value at about a 2x higher share price, and think that it could get back there as it deleverages its balance sheet, interest rates return to lower levels, and REITs recover from this bear market.

Canadian Net REIT (NET.UN:CA): Foreign Markets

Finally, we look at another REIT, but this time in Canada.

Canadian Net REIT just reported a very strong quarter, growing its FFO per share 9% so far this year.

Interest rates have already been cut substantially in Canada, and this is allowing the REIT to return to faster growth by funding new investments at large spreads over its cost of capital.

Even then, the REIT still remains discounted, trading at 8x FFO, or put differently a 12% FFO yield. It also pays a 6.5% dividend yield, all while retaining half of its cash flow to reinvest in growth.

Its American peers like Realty Income (O) are discounted as well, but Canadian Net REIT is even cheaper, despite growing a lot faster:

Canadian Net REIT Realty Income
FFO Multiple 8.3x 13.2x
FFO per Share Growth 9% 3-4%

We think that this is because Canadian Net REIT is a lot smaller in size and focuses on Canada, and as a result, it gets overlooked by most investors.

The beauty here is that because the valuation is so heavily discounted, investors can expect to earn solid returns from the yield and growth alone, and do not rely on multiple expansion.

But we estimate fair value at 12x FFO, which implies about 50% upside potential from here.

That's not without its risks, of course. Canadian Net REIT is a micro-cap with low liquidity, especially if you buy it in the US with the ticker CNNRF. The liquidity is better in Canada with the ticker NET.UN:CA, but it is still low.

The REIT also uses a bit more leverage than its US peers with an LTV of around 50%, which makes it more sensitive to interest rates. Given that we expect interest rates to continue to be cut, we view this as a positive and expect it to result in greater upside going forward.

Closing Note

The broad market indexes are historically expensive, but they hide pockets of weakness, especially in deep value stocks, niche sectors like REITs, and foreign markets.

This is where we are finding the steepest discounts this Black Friday and concentrating our efforts right now at High Yield Landlord.

data.title: Templeton Growth Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Global equity markets collectively advanced through the third quarter of 2025 as tariff anxieties gradually eased, central banks adopted a more accommodative policy stance, and leadership among technology-related stocks persisted.
  • Detractors: The fund (Advisor Class without sales charges) underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI All Country World Index-NR, for the quarter, primarily due to stock selection and underweights in the information technology (IT) and consumer discretionary sectors and stock selection in the materials sector.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in the health care sector, an underweight in the consumer staples sector and a lack of exposure to the real estate sector contributed to relative performance for the quarter.
  • Outlook: In broad terms, global equity markets have continued to shrug off geopolitical risks and macroeconomic uncertainty, driving many equity indexes to new highs. We see no immediate reason for this positive trend to reverse, though we remain vigilant in monitoring global developments. The key question for US and global equities is whether the market broadens beyond the current artificial intelligence (AI)-focused leadership.

Performance Review

  • Global equity markets collectively advanced through the third quarter of 2025 as tariff anxieties gradually eased, central banks adopted a more accommodative policy stance, and leadership among technology-related stocks persisted. July’s market tone improved amid clearer trade signals and generally resilient US corporate earnings; August saw equity gains despite episodic volatility around new tariff measures; and September capped the quarter with a US Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cut that steadied rate-sensitive assets and lifted risk appetite. The Fed lowered its target rate by 25 basis points, while the European Central Bank and the Bank of England held their policy rates steady; the Bank of Japan kept its key rate unchanged but continued its stance of edging toward policy normalization. Together, these dynamics, along with calmer rate volatility and encouraging corporate results, underpinned risk assets into quarter-end.
  • The fund (Advisor Class without sales charges) underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI All Country World Index-NR, for the quarter, primarily due to stock selection and underweights in the IT and consumer discretionary sectors and stock selection in the materials sector.
  • Global agricultural equipment producer CNH Industrial (CNH) was a top detractor for the quarter, as its shares were impacted by weak guidance from peers Deere (not held by the fund) and Caterpillar (not held by the fund) amid renewed US tariff pressures. Despite near-term softness, we believe CNH remains a mispriced cyclical company with meaningful self-help potential. In our view, management’s focus on pricing discipline, manufacturing quality, and dealer network upgrades— alongside integration of precision agriculture technology—should help drive structurally higher profit margins and improved through-cycle cash returns. Although recent results underscore short-term headwinds, we view these risks as largely priced in and continue to hold a constructive long-term outlook.
  • Stock selection in the health care sector, an underweight in the consumer staples sector and a lack of exposure to the real estate sector contributed to relative performance for the quarter.
  • Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) (TSM) delivered notably strong results. Asia ex Japan was a standout region during the quarter, as semiconductor-heavy markets surged amid renewed demand expectations. Our position in TSMC benefited directly from this trend, as the company continued to demonstrate its leadership in advanced process technologies and its critical role in supplying the world’s most sophisticated AI and computing infrastructure. This exposure helped offset weakness elsewhere and reinforced our confidence in maintaining overweight positions in high-quality Asian companies supported by favorable structural demand.
  • Regionally, stock selection within the United States, particularly in the IT sector, was the most significant detractor from quarterly performance. Our holdings in Salesforce and Intuit, both viewed by the market as AI losers, underperformed meaningfully. At the same time, several large index constituents that we do not hold, notably Oracle and Tesla, rose sharply and contributed to relative underperformance.
  • In contrast, stock selection in Asia was strong, driven by positions in TSMC and other AI-related companies. European holdings were mixed, with aerospace and defense stocks providing some offset to weakness elsewhere.

Outlook

  • In broad terms, global equity markets have continued to shrug off geopolitical risks and macroeconomic uncertainty, driving many equity indexes to new highs. We see no immediate reason for this positive trend to reverse, though we remain vigilant in monitoring global developments. The key question for US and global equities is whether the market broadens beyond the current AI-focused leadership. Our view is that it is possible, and the fund should be reasonably well-positioned for such a rotation. We remain selective in the IT sector, with financials and health care among our preferred sectors.
  • In the Asia Pacific (APAC) region, we similarly maintain a bottom-up selective approach even as our outlook on APAC potentially improves. While not yet expensive, we believe regional valuations have increased and risk/reward adjustments remain key. Potential venues where we see mispricing opportunities include underappreciated non-AI technology companies in Taiwan, along with South Korean stocks.
  • In Europe, we believe the combination of policy support, structural investment and attractive valuations underpin a constructive outlook. Market breadth is another key feature, in our view, offering a more diversified potential opportunity set across industries and sectors such as luxury, health care, industrials and electrification technologies.
